qa_pairs.question: What are the two main application processes for the Resource Allocation Competition (RAC)?
qa_pairs.answer: The two main application processes are Resources for Research Groups (RRG), which includes Fast Track applications, and Research Platforms and Portals (RPP), which includes Annual Progress Reports.

qa_pairs.question: When do applications for the Resource Allocation Competition open?
qa_pairs.answer: Applications for the Resource Allocation Competition open on September 23, 2025.

qa_pairs.question: What is the deadline for submitting applications to the Resource Allocation Competition?
qa_pairs.answer: The deadline for submitting applications is November 4, 2025, at 11:59 p.m. Eastern Standard Time.

qa_pairs.question: Is it possible to get an extension on the application deadline for the Resource Allocation Competition?
qa_pairs.answer: No, an extension of the application deadline is not possible.

qa_pairs.question: What is the purpose of the Resource Allocation Competition (RAC)?
qa_pairs.answer: The Resource Allocation Competition (RAC) allows faculty members and their research groups to access compute, storage, and cloud resources beyond what can be obtained via the Rapid Access Service (RAS).

qa_pairs.question: Who oversees the Resource Allocation Competition (RAC)?
qa_pairs.answer: The Resource Allocation Competition (RAC) is overseen by the Resource Access Program Administrative Committee (RAPAC).

qa_pairs.question: What kind of representatives are part of the Resource Access Program Administrative Committee (RAPAC)?
qa_pairs.answer: The RAPAC includes representatives from all regional partners and each of the national system host sites.

qa_pairs.question: What should applicants do if they plan to request GPU or cloud resources?
qa_pairs.answer: Applicants planning to request GPU and/or cloud resources should attend one of the corresponding information sessions.

qa_pairs.question: What is strongly encouraged for first-time applicants to the Resource Allocation Competition?
qa_pairs.answer: First-time applicants are strongly encouraged to schedule a pre-submission consultation before October 31.

qa_pairs.question: What version of the application template must be used for the Resource Allocation Competition?
qa_pairs.answer: Applicants must use the most up-to-date version of the application template provided in the guide.

qa_pairs.question: What could happen if an applicant fails to use the most up-to-date application template?
qa_pairs.answer: Failing to use the most up-to-date application template may result in the rejection of your application.

qa_pairs.question: Where can applicants find the evaluation criteria for the Resource Allocation Competition?
qa_pairs.answer: Applicants can consult the evaluation criteria available in Appendix A for Resources for Research Groups and Appendix B for Research Platforms and Portals within this document.

qa_pairs.question: Are there other resources available for applicants to consult regarding the RAC?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, applicants may also wish to consult the RAC Frequently Asked Questions page and the Technical Glossary.

qa_pairs.question: Who should an applicant contact if they are unsure which application process is best for their project?
qa_pairs.answer: If an applicant doesn't know which application process is best for their project, they should email allocations@tech.alliancecan.ca.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary purpose of projects applying through the Resources for Research Groups (RRG) process?
qa_pairs.answer: The primary purpose of projects applying through the Resources for Research Groups (RRG) process is to conduct research requiring compute, storage, and cloud resources to meet their goals.

qa_pairs.question: For which types of projects should applicants consider the Research Platforms and Portals (RPP) application process instead of RRG?
qa_pairs.answer: Projects primarily needing persistent instances in the cloud to provide a service through a platform or a portal should apply through the RPP application process instead of RRG.

qa_pairs.question: Where can the RRG Evaluation Criteria and scoring matrix be found?
qa_pairs.answer: The RRG Evaluation Criteria and scoring matrix can be found in the Appendices at the end of this Guide.

qa_pairs.question: Who is eligible for the RRG Fast Track Application Process?
qa_pairs.answer: Users with an existing RRG award who meet the Fast Track eligibility are allowed to submit a lightweight progress report to renew their request for computational resources.

qa_pairs.question: When will Principal Investigators (PIs) with an active RRG allocation be notified about their Fast Track eligibility?
qa_pairs.answer: Principal Investigators (PIs) with an active allocation awarded through the RRG process will receive an email in September indicating whether they are eligible to Fast Track or not.

qa_pairs.question: What is the primary purpose of projects applying through the Research Platforms and Portals (RPP) process?
qa_pairs.answer: The primary purpose of projects applying through the Research Platforms and Portals (RPP) process is to provide a service through 'scientific gateways' that improve access to shared datasets, enhance existing online research tools and facilities, or advance national or international research collaborations.

qa_pairs.question: For which types of projects should applicants consider the Resources for Research Groups (RRG) application process instead of RPP?
qa_pairs.answer: Projects primarily needing compute resources in a cluster to conduct research should apply through the RRG process instead of RPP.

qa_pairs.question: What is a research platform or portal?
qa_pairs.answer: A research platform or portal is a set of community-developed tools, applications, and data that are integrated via a gateway or a suite of applications—usually in a graphical user interface—that is further customized to meet the needs of a community of users associated with a specific discipline.

qa_pairs.question: What resources do RPP projects typically involve?
qa_pairs.answer: RPP projects typically involve cloud resources, usually through the development of a front-end gateway on persistent virtual machines, with possible backend compute either through cloud compute nodes or job-based submission to the national clusters, and often include large databases.

qa_pairs.question: What are the two main requirements for projects applying to the Research Platforms and Portals (RPP) process?
qa_pairs.answer: Projects applying to RPP must provide a service to a larger research community via a set of cloud-based tools, applications, and/or data, enabling access to national computational resources via a common interface, and be able to develop, operate, and manage the proposed portal or platform with minimal support from the Federation.

qa_pairs.question: Where can the RPP Evaluation Criteria and scoring matrix be found?
qa_pairs.answer: The RPP Evaluation Criteria and scoring matrix can be found in the Appendices at the end of this Guide.
